summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between a scene from Three Kings about necessity driving human behavior and Steve Krug's concept of 'satisficing' in user experience design. The post argues that users don't carefully weigh all options—they choose the first reasonable one that meets their immediate needs. Atwood uses research on firefighters and other high-stakes decision makers to show this isn't laziness but a fundamental human behavior pattern. The core message is that designers must account for this reality rather than assuming users will invest time exploring all features. Designs built for idealized user behavior are destined to fail.
key_points: People do what is most necessary to them at any given moment, not what is optimal
Users satisfice—they choose the first reasonable option rather than the best one
Even high-stakes professionals like firefighters don't compare options; they take the first workable plan
Designs that assume significant user investment are doomed because they ignore actual behavior
Users have specific goals and will do only the absolute minimum necessary to achieve them
money_quotes: As in people do what is most necessary to them at any given moment.
Most of the time we don't choose the best option – we choose the first reasonable option, a strategy known as satisficing.
Make sure you're designing for what is necessary, rather than what is possible.
Designs that assume more investment on the part of the user are doomed, because they are designing for idealized behavior rather than actual behavior.
Users really don't care about your application – they have specific goals and will do only the absolute minimum necessary to achieve those goals.
The fire commanders didn't compare any options. They took the first reasonable plan that came to mind and did a quick mental test for problems.
key_insight: Design for how users actually behave—doing the minimum necessary to achieve their goals—not for how you wish they would engage with your carefully crafted features.
